moves.explanation: Controls the center immediately and opens lines for the bishop and queen. The most direct way to fight for central space from move one.

children.explanation: Black mirrors White's central claim, establishing a symmetric pawn tension that defines open game theory.
Develops a piece with tempo by attacking the e5 pawn, forcing Black to defend. Natural, efficient, and principled.
The most natural defense of e5, developing the knight toward the center.
The Ruy Lopez move. The bishop indirectly pressures e5 by targeting the knight that defends it, posing a long-term strategic threat.
The Morphy Defense — Black immediately challenges the bishop to declare its intentions, gaining space on the queenside.
Retreating maintains the pin on the Nc6 without exchanging, keeping long-term pressure on e5.
Black attacks the e4 pawn with tempo, accelerating development and preparing to castle.
White castles, safeguarding the king and preparing the Ruy Lopez bind with Re1 and c3.
Solid development preparing to castle. Black chooses safety over counterplay, entering the Closed Ruy.
Attacks the bishop immediately to gain queenside space, though it weakens the a5 square slightly.
The Open Ruy Lopez — Black grabs a pawn but must handle sharp tactical complications after d4.
The Steinitz Deferred — solid pawn support for e5 but blocks the dark-squared bishop.
Prepares d4 to build a strong pawn center, a core idea of the Ruy Lopez system.
The Berlin Defense — pragmatic and solid, directly countering e4. Favored at the top level for its endgame resilience.
White castles first, inviting the Berlin endgame which White accepts for a structural advantage.
Black takes the pawn, entering the famous Berlin endgame — drawish but deeply strategic.
The Steinitz Defense — very solid, reinforcing e5, but it gives up the bishop pair and falls slightly behind in development.
Preparing the central thrust d4 to challenge Black's solid but passive setup.
Black develops and attacks e4 before White can build with d4.

moves.explanation: Controls the center and opens lines. The foundation of the Italian Game.

children.explanation: Black establishes a central pawn, contesting the center symmetrically.
Attacks e5 immediately while developing. The most natural follow-up.
Defends e5 with a developing move.
The Italian bishop — aims at the vulnerable f7 square and controls the center diagonally. A classical and aggressive piece placement.
The Giuoco Piano — Black mirrors White's diagonal control, eyeing f2. Leads to rich strategic battles.
Prepares d4 to challenge the center. The main line of the Italian, building a classical pawn center.
Black develops and attacks e4, gaining time before White can play d4.
Solid support of e5, though slightly passive. Black prepares queenside development.
White castles first for king safety before committing to a pawn structure. Flexible and solid.
Black counterattacks e4 while completing development.
The quiet Italian — White opts for a slower build-up, avoiding early pawn battles.
Natural development, preparing to castle.
Two Knights Defense — aggressive counterplay, attacking e4 and inviting White to overextend.
Attacks f7 immediately — the most aggressive response, leading to sharp tactical play.
The correct counterattack — Black must challenge the center or fall into a passive position.
Quiet and solid — White avoids the tactical Two Knights complications, aiming for steady development.
Black develops the bishop to its best diagonal, transposing to a Giuoco Piano type position.
Hungarian Defense — very solid but passive. Black avoids Italian complications at the cost of activity.
Preparing d4 to exploit Black's passive setup with a strong central advance.

moves.explanation: Controls the center, starting one of the most aggressive openings in chess history.

children.explanation: Black claims central space symmetrically.
The King's Gambit! White offers a pawn to destroy Black's central pawn and open the f-file for attack. Aggressive and romantic.
Accepting the gambit — Black takes the pawn but must weather White's attacking initiative.
Develops with tempo and prepares to castle, building attacking pressure against the f4 pawn and the kingside.
Holding the extra pawn aggressively with a space grab, though it weakens the kingside.
Solid development, consolidating the extra pawn before counterattacking.
Counter-developing, trading activity for the extra material.
The Bishop's Gambit — White aims directly at f7, combining development with immediate attacking threats.
Defends the extra pawn and threatens g4, but invites a sharp battle.
Active development, attacking e4 rather than holding the extra pawn passively.
The Falkbeer Counter-Gambit — Black declines and attacks the center directly with d5, counter-gambiting.
Developing naturally before committing to any pawn captures.
Solid structure, keeping the bishop and preventing White's center from advancing.
Developing toward the center, pressuring e4.
White takes the pawn, but Black gets strong compensation with the active Bc5 bishop.
Recapturing with the bishop, maintaining the diagonal and central pressure.
The Falkbeer Counter-Gambit — aggressive counterplay in the center rather than accepting the gambit.
White accepts, opening the position and leading to complex tactical battles.

moves.explanation: Controls the center with the d-pawn, the starting move for the London System.

children.explanation: Black also controls the center, entering a symmetrical pawn structure.
Developing the knight before committing the bishop, keeping options open.
Black mirrors White's knight development.
The London bishop — placed outside the pawn chain early, it creates a solid, hard-to-attack structure. The defining move of the system.
Solid and flexible, supporting d5 and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop.
Completing the bishop chain and preparing to develop Bd3, building a solid but slightly passive structure.
Black challenges the London bishop directly, trading it to eliminate White's best-placed piece.
Solid development, preparing to castle and maintaining flexibility.
Reinforcing d4 and preparing a possible queenside expansion.
Aggressive counterplay against d4, the most dynamic response to the London.
Solid and principled, not rushing to capture but maintaining the structure.
Developing and pressuring d4, maintaining the tension.
Solid, preparing to develop and castle.
The Slav setup — reinforcing d5 and preparing a solid defensive structure.
Consolidating the London structure before further development.
Black develops the bishop actively before playing e6, a key idea in the London setup.
White mirrors with the London bishop, reaching a symmetric position.
Black develops the knight first, keeping the d5 square flexible.
Developing naturally before defining the pawn structure.
Black prepares a King's Indian type fianchetto setup.
White deploys the London bishop, adapting the system to the King's Indian structure.
Solid and flexible, allowing multiple pawn setups.
White sets up the London bishop, targeting the queenside.

moves.explanation: Controls the center with the d-pawn, the foundation of Queen's pawn openings.

children.explanation: Black contests the center directly with a symmetric response.
The Queen's Gambit — White attacks d5 to displace it and gain central control. Not actually a gambit since 2...dxc4 can't be held.
The QGD — Black firmly declines, supporting d5 with the e-pawn. Solid but the c8 bishop is temporarily blocked.
Developing the knight while maintaining pressure on d5. The main line continuation.
Developing toward the center, preparing to castle.
The Classical QGD — pins the knight against the queen, creating pressure on d5 and eyeing a long-term attack.
Solid development, preparing to castle and unpinning the knight at the right moment.
Completing queenside development before castling, building a solid pawn chain.
Preparing to challenge with c5, a dynamic approach to free Black's game.
Exchanging to open the c-file and prepare queenside expansion.
Immediately challenging the pin — risky as it weakens the kingside but gains the bishop pair.
White doubles Black's pawns, gaining a structural advantage in exchange for the bishop pair.
A quieter approach — developing without pinning immediately, aiming for solid positional play.
Solid and flexible, preparing to castle kingside.
The London-QGD hybrid — White develops the bishop outside the pawn chain.
Developing the knight first before committing the queenside knight.
Matching development, preparing to castle.
Developing the queenside knight, building toward the classical QGD setup.

moves.explanation: The first step of the Queen's Gambit — controlling the center with a strong pawn.

children.explanation: Black contests the center symmetrically.
Offering the Queen's Gambit, attacking d5 to gain central dominance.
Accepting the gambit — Black takes the pawn and must return it or defend it carefully, ceding the center temporarily.
The most aggressive response — White immediately builds a massive center, knowing Black cannot hold the pawn long-term.
Black fights for counterplay in the center rather than trying to hold the extra pawn.
Developing and attacking e5, forcing Black to make a structural decision.
White advances the center pawn aggressively, gaining space.
Developing while defending the c4 pawn, an ambitious try.
Developing naturally before dealing with the c4 pawn.
Developing with flexibility — White will regain the c4 pawn easily while maintaining central tension.
Developing naturally before returning or defending the extra pawn.
Solid — preparing Bxc4 to recapture the gambit pawn with tempo.
Developing the queenside knight, maintaining pressure on the center.
Defending the extra pawn with b5, though this concedes the center.
Preparing to recapture on c4 while developing solidly.

moves.explanation: Central control with the d-pawn, the foundation of the Catalan.

children.explanation: Black develops actively, keeping the center flexible.
The Queen's Gambit move, attacking the center and preparing a fianchetto setup.
Solid and flexible — Black supports a future d5 and prepares normal development.
The Catalan move — White fianchettoes the bishop to exert long-term diagonal pressure on the queenside, one of the most strategically rich setups in chess.
Black establishes the classical QGD pawn structure, contesting the center.
The Catalan bishop — on g2 it exerts permanent pressure down the long diagonal, targeting d5 and c6.
Solid development, preparing to castle and reinforcing the center.
The Open Catalan — Black accepts the pawn to open lines, but White gets strong diagonal pressure.
Checking to disrupt White's development, a sharp alternative.
Challenging d4 immediately rather than supporting d5, a dynamic approach.
Completing the fianchetto before dealing with the c5 challenge.
Black exchanges in the center, opening the position for active piece play.

moves.explanation: The English Opening — White controls d5 from the flank, delaying the central pawns for a flexible hypermodern setup.

children.explanation: Black immediately occupies the center, fighting for the initiative.
Developing the knight toward the center, preparing to contest e5 and d5.
Active development, controlling the center and preparing to castle.
Completing development and maintaining flexible central pressure.
Preparing the kingside fianchetto for long-term diagonal pressure against Black's center.
The Four Knights English — White claims a full central pawn duo for a more classical game.
Solid development, mirroring White's knight.
Preparing the fianchetto, the most popular continuation in the English.
Developing before committing to a fianchetto, keeping the structure flexible.
Black develops flexibly, waiting to see White's setup before committing.
Developing toward the center with natural piece development.
Black contests the center directly, challenging c4.
Solid and flexible, preparing d5 or b6 setups.
Preparing a King's Indian style fianchetto against the English.
Developing before committing the queenside knight.
Solid setup, preparing d5 and normal piece development.
Fianchetto setup, aiming for a King's Indian type position.
The Symmetrical English — Black mirrors White's setup, leading to rich strategic battles with no clear weaknesses.
Developing naturally while maintaining the symmetry.
Black mirrors with symmetrical knight development.
Active development before deciding on the knight's optimal square.

moves.explanation: Central control — the starting point of the Scotch Game.

children.explanation: Black claims equal central space.
Developing with tempo, the natural third move.
Defending e5 and developing.
The Scotch Game — White immediately opens the center on move 3, avoiding the long theory of the Ruy Lopez.
Black accepts the offered pawn tension, opening the position.
Recapturing with the knight, centralizing it powerfully.
Active development, attacking e4 and inviting tactical complications.
The main line — doubling Black's c-pawns in exchange for rapid development.
Defending e4 and developing, leading to a complex middlegame.
Classical development — the bishop pressures the knight on d4 and aims at the long diagonal.
Defending the knight and preparing queenside castling for a sharp attacking game.
Retreating the knight to a safer square, keeping the bishop pair.
The Scotch Gambit declined — an unusual early queen move hoping for tricks.
Threatening Nc7+ and d6+, forcing Black to deal with the threat immediately.

moves.explanation: Central control — the foundation of the Four Knights.

children.explanation: Black mirrors with equal central control.
Developing with tempo.
Defending e5 naturally.
White develops the second knight, maintaining symmetry and heading toward the Four Knights.
Black completes the Four Knights setup — all four knights developed, solid and principled.
The Spanish Four Knights — applying Ruy Lopez pressure, targeting Nc6 and threatening to win the e5 pawn.
Symmetrical response — Black mirrors White's pin, leading to complex double-pin positions.
Castling early for safety before the position opens up.
Attacking Bb4 while centralizing the knight aggressively.
Black jumps to the central outpost, gaining space and attacking Bb5.
Retreating to a more active square, targeting f7 in the classical style.
Immediate central challenge — White opens the game sharply rather than developing pieces first.
Black accepts, opening the position for tactical play.

moves.explanation: White claims the center — Black's plan is to fight back asymmetrically.

children.explanation: The Sicilian! Black fights for the d4 square without mirroring with e5, creating an imbalanced position right away.
White develops and prepares d4 to open the center.
Controlling e5 and preparing Nf6, the most flexible Sicilian move.
White opens the center, the critical moment — Black must capture to avoid falling behind.
Black exchanges to open the c-file, the key counterplay mechanism in all Sicilians.
White recaptures with the knight, centralizing it powerfully.
Developing with tempo, attacking e4 and preparing to castle.
Defending e4 and developing.
The Najdorf move! Stops Bb5+ and prepares b5 queenside expansion — the most popular and dynamic Sicilian variation.
Gains space and kicks the Nd4, the English Attack is met with a central counter.
Attacking the Be3 bishop, a sharp tactical approach popular in the Najdorf.
Solid development, preparing e5 or b5 expansion.
Supporting d6 and preparing to develop queenside pieces, the main classical response.
Preparing e5 immediately, a dynamic equalizing approach.
Counterattacking the center before White's attack gains momentum.
Solid development, preparing e5 after White commits.

moves.explanation: White stakes a claim in the center.

children.explanation: The Sicilian — asymmetric fight for the d4 square.
Preparing d4.
Flexible — controlling e5 and allowing multiple Sicilian setups.
Opening the center.
Opening the c-file for Black's counterplay.
Recapturing with the knight.
Developing actively.
Defending e4.
The Dragon! Black fianchettoes the bishop to g7, creating a powerful diagonal that will blast open after ...d5 or ...e5. The most double-edged Sicilian.
Completing the Dragon fianchetto — the g7 bishop becomes Black's most powerful piece.
The Yugoslav Attack — White prepares a kingside pawn storm with g4-g5.
Quieter development, avoiding the sharpest Yugoslav lines.
Completing the fianchetto despite White's aggressive setup.
White kicks the Nf6, gaining space but creating a weak d5 square for Black to exploit.
Developing solidly before launching the kingside attack.
Completing the Dragon fianchetto.

moves.explanation: White claims the center.

children.explanation: The Sicilian — asymmetric central fight.
Preparing d4.
The Classical Sicilian — Black develops the knight immediately to c6, maintaining flexibility about where the d-pawn will go.
Opening the center.
Opening the c-file for Black's queenside counterplay.
Recapturing centrally.
Active development, attacking e4 and keeping options open for the d-pawn.
Defending e4 and developing.
Solid — supporting e5 and preparing queenside development, heading toward a Scheveningen-type position.
Aggressive — kicking the Nd4 and claiming central space immediately.
The Dragon setup — fianchettoing the bishop for long-term diagonal pressure.
Solid — controlling e5 and preparing Nf6 with a Najdorf-type structure.
Defending e4 and developing.
Developing with tempo, attacking e4.
Solid setup, preparing Be7 and castling.

moves.explanation: White claims the center aggressively.

children.explanation: The French Defense — preparing d5 to challenge the center. Solid but the c8 bishop can be temporarily restricted.
White builds a strong pawn center.
The key French move — challenging the center immediately and leading to rich strategic battles around the pawn chain.
Defending e4 and developing, the Classical and Winawer lines branch from here.
Natural development, attacking e4 and heading toward the Classical French.
The Classical French — pinning the knight to increase pressure on d5 and e5.
Unpinning solidly, preparing to castle kingside. The main Classical French response.
Burns the center immediately, trading pawn tension for piece activity.
The Advance Variation — White gains space but creates a fixed pawn chain that Black will attack with c5.
The knight retreats to support c5 and e5 breaks, the main French Advance plan.
The Winawer Variation — pinning the Nc3, creating maximum imbalance and one of the most theoretically dense French lines.
White advances, creating a space advantage and a fixed pawn chain Black will attack.
White exchanges to simplify, though Black gets an active position.
Rubinstein Variation — rare but leads to a unique position after Nxe4.
White recaptures centrally, gaining space while Black plans active counterplay.
The Tarrasch Variation — defending e4 without blocking the c1 bishop, a flexible and positional approach.
Active development, attacking e4 and pressuring the center.
White advances, gaining space and starting the Tarrasch pawn chain battle.
The key French move — retreating the knight to support c5 and attack the pawn chain from the base.
Developing more pieces before advancing, keeping more flexibility.
Black strikes at the base of the pawn chain immediately.
Immediate counter-attack on d4 — the most aggressive Tarrasch approach.
White simplifies, getting an IQP position with active piece play.
Developing before capturing, maintaining central tension.
The Exchange Variation — White releases central tension, aiming for a symmetrical position that is considered drawish but can be tricky.
Recapturing to maintain the symmetric pawn structure.

moves.explanation: White claims the center.

children.explanation: The Caro-Kann — preparing d5 with solid pawn support. Unlike the French, the c8 bishop stays active.
White builds a strong pawn center.
The key Caro-Kann move — challenging the center with c6 already supporting d5, which is more solid than in the French.
The Classical Caro-Kann — defending e4 with the knight.
Exchanging to reach the Classical Caro-Kann — Black gets excellent piece activity after Nxe4.
Recapturing centrally with the knight.
The Classical Caro-Kann move — the bishop develops outside the pawn chain before ...e6, a key advantage over the French.
Solid — preparing to develop without committing the bishop immediately.
The Gurgenidze System — Black prepares a fianchetto, aiming for a King's Indian type structure.
Developing naturally before dealing with Black's setup.
Aggressive — White prepares a kingside attack immediately.
The Exchange Variation — White simplifies the center but Black recaptures with good piece activity.
Black recaptures with the c-pawn, maintaining a symmetric structure.
Attacking the d5 pawn immediately — the Panov-Botvinnik Attack, leading to IQP positions.
Developing solidly before creating pawn pressure.
The Advance Variation — White gains space but must defend the pawn chain against ...c5 and ...Bf5.
The key Caro-Kann advantage — Black develops the c8 bishop outside the pawn chain before playing ...e6.
Immediate counterplay against d4, the most aggressive Advance Caro-Kann response.
White develops without d4 first, maintaining flexibility.
Black stakes the center anyway.
Developing naturally, preparing d4.
Building the pawn center, transposing to classical Caro-Kann lines.

moves.explanation: White builds a queen's pawn center — Black will allow it and then attack it.

children.explanation: Developing actively, controlling e4 while keeping the center flexible for the KID setup.
White builds a broad pawn center.
The King's Indian — Black prepares to fianchetto the bishop, allowing White a big center only to attack it violently later.
Developing and reinforcing the center.
The KID bishop — placed on g7 it becomes Black's most powerful piece, pressuring along the long diagonal and supporting kingside attacks.
White completes the ideal pawn center — now Black must attack it decisively.
Solid — supporting e5 and preparing the KID's classic ...e5 break to challenge White's center.
Natural development before committing to a specific system.
Black castles, preparing the key ...e5 break and the kingside attack.
The Classical KID — solid development, White prepares to fight the ...e5 break.
Preparing for queenside castling and a sharp kingside attack with the Sämisch system.
Preventing ...Ng4, the Averbakh with h3 — solid but slightly slow.
White develops the bishop early, heading for the Classical KID.
Castling and preparing the ...e5 break.

moves.explanation: White builds the center — the Grünfeld allows it temporarily.

children.explanation: Developing actively.
Extending the center.
Preparing the Grünfeld fianchetto — Black will allow a massive White center and then demolish it with ...d5.
White reinforces the center.
The Grünfeld move! Black directly challenges the center before completing development — a provocative and principled hypermodern concept.
White accepts the challenge, heading for the main Grünfeld lines.
Black recaptures, inviting White to build a huge center that Black will attack with ...c5 and the g7 bishop.
White builds the classical Grünfeld center — e4+d4, the maximum central ambition.
Black destroys the key defender of the center, aiming to prove that White's pawns are overextended.
White recaptures, maintaining the pawn center but with a structural weakness on c3.
Developing the bishop with tempo instead of recapturing, an ambitious approach.
Developing before expanding — a quieter Grünfeld approach.
Completing the fianchetto, the Grünfeld bishop becomes Black's key attacking piece.
A quieter approach that avoids the main theoretical lines.
Completing the fianchetto and preparing ...dxc4 or ...c6.
Grabbing the pawn before White can recapture, leading to complex positions.

moves.explanation: White builds the center.

children.explanation: Developing actively, keeping options open.
Expanding the center.
Solid — supporting a future d5 and preparing to develop queenside pieces.
White develops the knight.
The Nimzo-Indian! Black pins the Nc3, preventing White from maintaining a classical center without conceding the bishop pair. One of the most theoretically rich and strategically deep openings.
Solid — White reinforces the center and prepares Bd3 and Nge2.
Castling before deciding on the pin — safe and flexible.
Immediate challenge against d4, the most dynamic Nimzo response.
The Classical Variation — White avoids doubled pawns by defending the c3 knight indirectly.
Castling first, keeping the position flexible.
Counterattacking d4 immediately before White can solidify the center.
The Sämisch Variation — White forces the issue immediately, accepting doubled pawns if Black takes.
Black trades the bishop for doubled pawns — giving White the bishop pair but saddling them with a structural weakness.
White recaptures, accepting the doubled c-pawns in exchange for the bishop pair and central control.
Retreating the bishop, maintaining the pin without giving up the bishop pair.
Developing naturally after Black retreats the bishop.

moves.explanation: White controls the center with d4.

children.explanation: The Dutch Defense — an aggressive and imbalanced response. Black fights for e4, stakes a kingside claim, but weakens the e6 and h5 squares.
White prepares a fianchetto, aiming the bishop down the long diagonal to put pressure on Black's kingside setup.
Developing naturally, reinforcing e4 control.
Completing the fianchetto — the bishop will target Black's kingside and apply pressure on the long diagonal.
Solid structure, preparing Be7 and castling. The Classical Dutch setup.
The Leningrad Dutch — Black also fianchettoes, creating a unique double-fianchetto battle.
Solid — supporting f5 and preparing to develop normally.
Completing the fianchetto.
White extends the center before developing the kingside.
Developing actively.
Preparing the fianchetto, the most popular continuation against the Dutch.
Developing the queenside knight before the fianchetto.
Simple development before committing to a pawn structure.
Developing symmetrically.
Solid Dutch structure.

moves.explanation: White builds the center.

children.explanation: Black contests the center symmetrically.
The Queen's Gambit.
The Slav Defense — supporting d5 with c6 instead of e6, keeping the c8 bishop free and avoiding the French bishop problem.
White develops and maintains pressure on d5.
Developing with tempo and preparing to castle.
Developing toward the Slav's main line.
Accepting the gambit — Black can hold the pawn with ...b5, unlike the QGA.
Solid — supporting the center while keeping the c8 bishop active.
Preparing ...b5 to accept the gambit while controlling b5 first.
Solid — supporting d4 and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop.
Solid Slav setup, preparing to develop both bishops.
Developing the bishop actively before playing ...e6 — a key Slav idea.
White develops the knight without committing the queenside knight.
Developing naturally.
Developing and entering classical Slav territory.
Solid approach, reinforcing d4.

moves.explanation: White claims the center.

children.explanation: The Scandinavian — Black immediately challenges e4 on move 1, accepting an early pawn trade but gaining quick piece activity.
White captures, and now Black's response defines the opening.
Recapturing with the queen — Black gets the pawn back immediately but must relocate the queen when White plays Nc3.
Developing with tempo, attacking the queen.
The main retreat — the queen is active on a5, eyeing the e1-a5 diagonal and preparing ...Nf6 and ...c6.
White builds the center while Black must show how the Qa5 compensates for the lost tempo.
Developing naturally before building the center.
Less common — the queen develops to a central square, preparing ...Nf6 and ...c6.
Natural development.
Building the center.
The Modern Scandinavian — Black doesn't recapture immediately, developing with tempo instead and planning to regain the pawn later.
Holding the pawn and building the center.
Recapturing the pawn after White commits the d-pawn.
Preparing a fianchetto instead of recapturing, a dynamic approach.
White advances, trying to keep the extra pawn.
Challenging the d5 pawn's defender and preparing to regain material.
Solid — attacking d5 while developing.

moves.explanation: White claims the center.

children.explanation: Black mirrors with equal central control.
White attacks e5 — the Petrov counters by attacking e4 in turn.
The Petrov Defense — instead of defending e5, Black counterattacks e4. Solid and rock-solid at the top level, with many draws among elite players.
White captures — but now Black must be careful with the sequence to equalize.
The critical Petrov move — kicking the Ne5 before recapturing on e4, to avoid losing material.
Retreating the knight, the safe main line.
Now Black recaptures safely — the Petrov sequence complete, reaching a roughly equal position.
White builds the center, the main Petrov main line continuation.
Developing, preparing to contest the central knight.
An aggressive but unusual knight retreat, trying to keep more tension.
Black recaptures the pawn now that the knight has moved.
White tries to avoid the Petrov main line with an early center advance.
Black accepts, entering a Scotch-type position.
White advances aggressively, kicking the Nf6 and gaining space.
Recapturing centrally, entering symmetrical Scotch-type play.

moves.explanation: White claims the center.

children.explanation: Black mirrors.
Attacking e5.
Defending e5.
The Ruy Lopez — targeting Nc6.
The Berlin Defense — counterattacking e4 immediately rather than defending the pin. Became famous for its extreme solidity after Kramnik used it to defeat Kasparov.
White castles — the critical Berlin endgame follows after Nxe4.
Black captures, entering the famous Berlin endgame — White will force the queens off the board but Black gets a rock-solid fortress.
White attacks immediately, leading to the main Berlin endgame after Re1.
Retreating the knight — the Berlin endgame structure begins, a deeply strategic position.
Developing solidly, keeping the knight on e4 temporarily.
Attacking the Ne4 directly, a sharp alternative to d4.
The knight retreats, and the Berlin endgame structure takes shape.
The Anti-Berlin — White avoids the endgame, choosing a slower positional approach.
Active development — the bishop targets f2 and the center.
Solid — reinforcing e5 and preparing queenside development.

moves.explanation: White claims the center — the Pirc allows it and then attacks it.

children.explanation: The first Pirc move — flexible, supporting e5 later and preparing g6. Black invites White to build a big center.
White builds a strong pawn center.
Developing actively, attacking e4 and preparing the fianchetto.
Defending e4 and developing, the main Pirc continuation.
The key Pirc move — fianchettoing the bishop to g7 where it will attack the White center from long range.
The Classical System — solid development, preparing Qd2 and castling queenside for a direct kingside attack.
Completing the fianchetto — the Pirc bishop on g7 is Black's key attacking piece.
Preparing queenside castling and a direct kingside attack, the Austrian or Classical setup.
The Sämisch-type approach — preparing g4-g5 for a kingside pawn storm.
The Austrian Attack — the most aggressive response to the Pirc, aiming for f5 and a massive kingside attack.
Completing the fianchetto, preparing ...c5 or ...O-O counterplay against White's attack.
Solid development, avoiding the sharpest lines.
Completing the fianchetto.
White develops without committing to Nc3, keeping flexibility.
Heading for the Pirc fianchetto setup.
Solid development, supporting e5 and preparing a different setup.

moves.explanation: White builds the center.

children.explanation: Developing actively.
Expanding the center.
Solid and flexible, preparing d5 or b6 setups.
White develops before committing to Nc3, which would allow the Nimzo-Indian.
The Queen's Indian! Black fianchettoes the c8 bishop to b7, controlling e4 from the flank — a hypermodern masterpiece.
White also fianchettoes — both sides aim their bishops at the long diagonals, leading to deep positional battles.
Completing the QID fianchetto — the Bb7 battles White's Bg2 for control of the long diagonal.
A provocative alternative — the bishop goes to a6 to trade off White's key Bg2 bishop.
White develops the queenside knight, reaching Nimzo-Indian territory if Black plays ...Bb4.
Completing the QID fianchetto and pressuring e4.
Transposing to the Nimzo-Indian with Bb4, a flexible and strong choice.
Solid — supporting d4 and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop.
Completing the QID setup.
Checking to disrupt White's development before completing the fianchetto.
